title: A One-in-a-Million Supernova Appears Five Times — and It Might Settle the Universe's Biggest Debate
summary: For nearly a century, we've known the universe is expanding, but the exact speed — called the Hubble constant — remains stubbornly uncertain. Two main methods give conflicting results, a puzzle known as the Hubble tension. Now, an international team has stumbled upon an extraordinarily rare cosmic event that could break the deadlock: a superluminous supernova that appears not once, but five times in the sky. Dubbed SN Winny, this stellar explosion sits about 10 billion light-years away. Its light passes by two foreground galaxies whose immense gravity acts as a cosmic lens, bending the light into multiple paths. Each path is a slightly different length, so the supernova's five images reach Earth at different times. By precisely measuring these time delays, scientists can calculate the Hubble constant directly — no cosmic distance ladder required. "The chance of finding a superluminous supernova perfectly aligned with a suitable gravitational lens is lower than one in a million," says Sherry Suyu of the Technical University of Munich. Her team spent six years compiling a list of promising lenses before SN Winny matched in August 2025. Using the Large Binocular Telescope in Arizona, researchers captured the first high-resolution color image of the system: two lensing galaxies surrounded by five bluish dots. Unlike most lensed supernovae, which are magnified by complex galaxy clusters, SN Winny's lens is just two individual galaxies with surprisingly smooth mass distributions. "The overall simplicity of the system offers an exciting opportunity to measure the universe's expansion rate with high accuracy," explains team member Allan Schweinfurth. Current methods disagree. The cosmic distance ladder builds step-by-step from nearby objects, accumulating small errors. The cosmic microwave background method relies on models of the early universe. A lensed supernova, however, is a one-step measurement with entirely different uncertainties. As Stefan Taubenberger, lead author of the identification study, notes, "Unlike the cosmic distance ladder, this is a one-step method, with fewer and completely different sources of systematic uncertainties." Astronomers worldwide are now monitoring SN Winny with ground- and space-based telescopes. The data could finally reconcile the dueling numbers and refine our understanding of cosmic evolution. In a field where a single observation can overturn decades of theory, SN Winny might just be the lucky break cosmologists have been waiting for.
why_it_matters: The Hubble constant tells us not only the age of the universe but also how it will end — a precise number could reveal unknown physics or refine models of dark energy, and SN Winny might be the key that finally settles a 100-year-old debate.

keywords.explanation: The number that describes how fast the universe is expanding; its exact value has been debated for decades.
The disagreement between two precise methods of measuring the universe's expansion rate, suggesting something might be wrong with our understanding.
An extremely bright stellar explosion, 10 to 100 times more luminous than a typical supernova.
A massive object (like a galaxy) whose gravity bends and splits light from a background source, acting like a natural telescope.
The differences in arrival time of light that travels along multiple bent paths from the same cosmic event.
Errors that come from the way a measurement is set up, rather than random chance, and can bias the result.

questions.question: What is the name of the superluminous supernova that appears five times?
How far away is SN Winny from Earth?
Which telescope captured the first high-resolution color image of the lens system?
Why is SN Winny's gravitational lens considered unusually simple?
options: It involves a cluster of galaxies
It comprises just two individual galaxies with smooth mass distributions
It is a single galaxy lens
questions.correct_answer: It comprises just two individual galaxies with smooth mass distributions

questions.question: What is the key advantage of using a lensed supernova to measure the Hubble constant, according to Stefan Taubenberger?
options: It relies on the cosmic distance ladder
It is a one-step method with different systematic uncertainties
It uses the cosmic microwave background
questions.correct_answer: It is a one-step method with different systematic uncertainties

questions.question: How long did the team compile a list of promising lenses before SN Winny matched?

background_read: The question of how fast the universe expands began with Edwin Hubble (the American astronomer who discovered the expanding universe in 1929). Since then, scientists have refined the Hubble constant using a 'cosmic distance ladder' that starts with nearby Cepheid variable stars and extends to distant Type Ia supernovae.

In parallel, the Planck satellite (a European Space Agency mission that mapped the afterglow of the Big Bang from 2009 to 2013) measured tiny fluctuations in the cosmic microwave background to calculate the expansion rate based on known physics of the early universe. Shockingly, the two methods differ by about 8%, a conflict that has persisted for over a decade and is known as the Hubble tension.

Gravitational lensing was first confirmed during a 1919 solar eclipse, validating Einstein's general relativity. Today, astronomers use it to study dark matter and to magnify distant galaxies. The Large Binocular Telescope (one of the world's highest-resolution optical telescopes, located on Mount Graham in Arizona) helped capture SN Winny and its lens in striking detail.

If the time delays between the five images can be measured precisely, they provide a direct geometry-based measurement, sidestepping the accumulated uncertainties of the distance ladder and the modeling assumptions of the cosmic microwave background approach. This rare find could finally break the deadlock that has divided cosmology.
Article_Structure: LEAD: The Hubble constant remains uncertain, with two methods giving conflicting results (Hubble tension).
└─ specific: 'a puzzle known as the Hubble tension'
KEY EVENT: A superluminous supernova (SN Winny) was discovered that appears five times due to gravitational lensing.
├─ WHO: international team led by Sherry Suyu, Stefan Taubenberger, et al.
├─ WHERE: about 10 billion light-years away, lensed by two foreground galaxies
└─ METHOD: measure time delays between images to calculate Hubble constant directly, bypassing cosmic distance ladder
├─ ONE SIDE: cosmic distance ladder (builds step-by-step, accumulates errors)
├─ OTHER SIDE: cosmic microwave background (relies on early-universe models)
└─ NEW METHOD: lensed supernova — a one-step measurement with different systematic uncertainties
EVIDENCE: Team spent six years compiling lens candidates; Large Binocular Telescope captured image; simplicity of two-galaxy lens boosts accuracy.
OPEN QUESTION: Will ongoing monitoring of SN Winny's multiple images reconcile the Hubble constant discrepancy?

perspectives.description: Astronomers see SN Winny as a golden ticket to resolve the Hubble tension without relying on distance ladders. A clean, one-step measurement could finally tell us whether the discrepancy is real, possibly pointing to new physics beyond the standard model.

Some caution that even a 'simple' two-galaxy lens might contain hidden mass clumps or dark matter substructure that could introduce errors. Finding more such systems is extremely rare, so one supernova alone may not be enough to overcome the history of conflicting measurements.

This approach adds a third, independent pillar to what has been a two-method standoff. Historically, breakthroughs in cosmology have come not from one perfect method but from cross-verifying multiple techniques; SN Winny could reveal systematic errors in either the distance ladder or the CMB approach.

If monitoring of SN Winny's five images yields a precise Hubble constant, astronomers will rush to find more lensed supernovae with upcoming survey telescopes like the Vera C. Rubin Observatory. Success here could open a new era of precise cosmic cartography and a deeper understanding of dark energy.
